official_docs_reference.title: Define tools - Apps SDK
official_docs_reference.relevant_point: Tool planning should define names, descriptions, inputSchema, outputSchema, annotations, auth requirements, rate limits, and error handling expectations.

official_docs_reference.title: Build your MCP server - Apps SDK
official_docs_reference.relevant_point: MCP responses should use structuredContent and content; read-only tools should be marked with readOnlyHint.

official_docs_reference.title: Reference - Apps SDK
official_docs_reference.relevant_point: Declare outputSchema for structuredContent so clients can validate results and the model can reason about follow-up calls.

official_docs_reference.title: Security & Privacy - Apps SDK
official_docs_reference.relevant_point: Validate all inputs server-side even if the model provided them, and require human confirmation for irreversible operations.

findings.title: Error tool results violate the declared outputSchema
findings.evidence: OBJECT_OUTPUT_SCHEMA requires structuredContent fields ok, data, and boundary, but fail() returns ok, error, and boundary without data.
findings.risk: A real Apps/MCP client can validate structuredContent against outputSchema. Failure responses for forbidden or invalid inputs can fail client-side validation, weakening the fail-closed contract exactly when the tool needs to communicate a safe denial.
findings.recommended_fix: Either add an explicit error shape to every outputSchema, or normalize fail() to include data:null plus a schema-declared error field and update tests to validate both success and failure envelopes.

findings.title: Input schemas advertise additionalProperties:false but the local executor accepts extra fields
findings.evidence: Probe executeChatGptMcpTool('request_package_freeze', { reason:'schema probe', extra_unexpected:true }) returned ok=true and created a pending request, despite the descriptor declaring additionalProperties:false.
findings.risk: The server is not enforcing its own MCP contract. A model or malicious prompt could smuggle unexpected fields into draft/pending stores, increasing review ambiguity and contradicting the security requirement to validate all inputs server-side.
findings.recommended_fix: Add a generic schema guard before handler execution that rejects extra fields and validates required primitive fields for every tool, then add negative tests for extra properties on read-only, draft-only, and human-confirmation-required tools.

findings.title: Tool descriptors are shallow-copied and can be mutated by in-process consumers
findings.evidence: Probe mutated listChatGptMcpToolDescriptors()[0].security.provider_call_allowed and the exported global CHATGPT_MCP_TOOL_DESCRIPTORS reflected the mutation in the same process.
findings.risk: The local in-process server can have its published metadata altered by a consumer that mutates a listed descriptor. Even if handler behavior remains guarded, metadata drift can mislabel provider or endpoint safety and invalidate reports/tests in long-lived local processes.
findings.recommended_fix: Deep-freeze the descriptor constants and return deep clones from listChatGptMcpToolDescriptors(), or generate descriptors fresh from immutable definitions.

commands: node -e JSON.parse(...) for R2G-A through R2G-F reports and schema fixture
manual negative probe: forbidden provider / fake ids / missing required field
manual negative probe: extra unexpected field

next_recommended_task.reason: Fix outputSchema/error-envelope mismatch, enforce input schemas server-side, and deep-freeze/clone tool descriptors before any live connector preparation.
